questions.question:
A palindrome is a word that reads the same forwards and backwards. In a game of words, a player has the following two plates painted with letters:

  Plate 1: A    Plate 2: D

From the additional plates given in the options, which one of the combinations of additional plates would allow the player to construct a five-letter palindrome? The player should use all five plates exactly once. The plates can be rotated in their plane.

A. Q I Z
B. D I A
C. M I W
D. I I Z
questions.to_find: which set of 3 additional plates, combined with plates A and D, forms a 5-letter palindrome using all plates exactly once

understand.plain: A palindrome reads the same left-to-right and right-to-left. The player already holds plates A and D. They must pick 3 more plates so that all 5 letters can be arranged into a palindrome. Crucially, plates can be rotated in their plane, so a plate showing one letter may also represent another (for example, rotating an A by 180 degrees can look like a V, and rotating a D by 90 degrees can resemble a different shape). The key insight is that A rotated 180 degrees still looks like A, and D rotated gives a mirrored D. With the additional plates D, I, A the five letters become A, D, D, I, A and the arrangement A-D-I-D-A is a valid palindrome.

keywords.explain: a sequence that reads identically in both directions
a square tile can be turned 90, 180, or 270 degrees within its plane; some letters look like different letters when rotated
keywords.example: rotating a plate with M by 180 degrees produces a W

keywords.explain: positions 1 and 5 must match, positions 2 and 4 must match, position 3 (middle) can be anything
keywords.example: ABCBA has A at positions 1 and 5, B at positions 2 and 4

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='60' height='60' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>A</text><rect x='90' y='30' width='60' height='60' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>D</text><rect x='170' y='30' width='60' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>D</text><rect x='250' y='30' width='60' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>I</text><rect x='330' y='30' width='60' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='360' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>A</text><text x='40' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>given</text><text x='120' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>given</text><text x='200' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>+3</text><text x='280' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>+3</text><text x='360' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>+3</text><text x='210' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Arrangement: A - D - I - D - A (palindrome)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five letter tiles showing A, D (given) and D, I, A (added from option B) arranged to spell A-D-I-D-A, a palindrome.

given.aim: find 3 additional plates that, combined with plates A and D, allow construction of a 5-letter palindrome using all 5 plates exactly once

terms.meaning: a tile printed with the letter A; can be rotated in-plane
terms.example: rotating A by 180 degrees still reads as A (symmetric letter)
terms.connects: occupies one of the 5 positions in the palindrome

terms.meaning: a tile printed with the letter D; can be rotated in-plane
terms.example: used as D in the arrangement A-D-I-D-A
terms.connects: occupies one of the 5 positions in the palindrome

terms.meaning: positions must satisfy pos1=pos5 and pos2=pos4, with pos3 free
terms.connects: determines which pairs of letters are needed among the 5 plates

terms.meaning: a plate can be turned 90/180/270 degrees; some letters become other letters under rotation
terms.example: M rotated 180 degrees looks like W; I rotated any amount still looks like I
terms.connects: expands the effective letter set available from each physical plate

given.plan: List the letters available from the two given plates (A, D). For a 5-letter palindrome we need two matching pairs plus one middle letter. Check each option's 3 plates: can those 5 letters (given + option) be arranged as a palindrome? Option B gives D, I, A — total set is {A, D, D, I, A}. Pairs: (A,A) and (D,D), middle: I. Arrangement A-D-I-D-A is a palindrome.

steps.title: Identify palindrome requirements for 5 letters
steps.formula_raw: For a 5-letter palindrome: pos1=pos5, pos2=pos4, pos3=any
steps.apply: We need exactly two matched pairs of letters plus one singleton (middle). The two given plates supply A and D. We need the 3 additional plates to complete two pairs and provide a middle letter.
steps.note: Pairs can be formed only if a letter appears at least twice among all 5 plates.

steps.title: Check Option A: plates Q, I, Z
steps.formula_raw: Set = {A, D, Q, I, Z} — all distinct, no pairs possible
steps.apply: No two letters match, so no palindrome arrangement exists. Eliminate option A.
steps.note: A 5-letter palindrome requires at least one pair.

steps.title: Check Option B: plates D, I, A
steps.formula_raw: Set = {A, D, D, I, A} — pairs (A,A) and (D,D), middle I
steps.apply: Arrange as A-D-I-D-A. Reading forward: A D I D A. Reading backward: A D I D A. This is a valid palindrome using all 5 plates exactly once.

steps.title: Verify options C and D are invalid
steps.formula_raw: Option C: {A,D,M,I,W} — M and W can swap by 180-degree rotation (same plate), so actual distinct letters are at most {A,D,M,I}; still no two full pairs. Option D: {A,D,I,I,Z} — pair (I,I) only one pair; A and D unmatched, Z singleton — cannot form palindrome.
steps.apply: Neither C nor D can yield a 5-letter palindrome with all plates used exactly once.
steps.note: Option B remains the unique valid answer.

solution.result: Option B (plates D, I, A) — the five plates spell A-D-I-D-A, a valid 5-letter palindrome.

questions.formula_note: No standard algebraic formula applies; the solution relies on the structural definition of a palindrome (mirror symmetry of letter positions) and enumeration of letter pairs.

questions.question:
Which one of the options best describes the transformation of the 2-dimensional figure P to Q, and then to R, as shown?

(The figure shows an asymmetric arrow-like shape. P is the original. Q is obtained from P, then R is obtained from Q.)

A. Option_A: A clockwise rotation by 90 degrees about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the figure; A reflection along a horizontal line
B. Option_B: A counter clockwise rotation by 90 degrees about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the figure; A reflection along a horizontal line
C. Option_C: A clockwise rotation by 90 degrees about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the figure; A reflection along a vertical line
D. Option_D: A counter clockwise rotation by 90 degrees about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the figure; A reflection along a vertical line
questions.answer: A. Option_A: A clockwise rotation by 90 degrees about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the figure; A reflection along a horizontal line
questions.to_find: the pair of 2D transformations that maps figure P to Q and then Q to R

understand.plain: The problem shows a 2-dimensional figure that undergoes two successive transformations. The first transformation takes figure P to figure Q, and the second takes Q to R. By examining the orientation and facing direction of the asymmetric figure at each stage, we can determine whether a rotation was clockwise or counter-clockwise, and whether a reflection was along a horizontal or vertical axis. An asymmetric shape makes it unambiguous which operation was applied at each step.

keywords.explain: a rotation in the plane that turns the figure to the right when viewed from the front; a 90-degree clockwise rotation maps the top of the figure to the right
keywords.example: an upward-pointing arrow after a 90-degree clockwise rotation points to the right

keywords.explain: a flip about a left-right axis; the top of the figure moves to the bottom and vice versa, while left and right stay the same
keywords.example: a capital letter P reflected horizontally becomes a P flipped upside down

keywords.explain: applying two transformations in sequence; the result depends on the order of operations
keywords.example: rotate then reflect gives a different final image than reflect then rotate in general

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='120' height='120' rx='8' class='bg'/><polygon points='70,35 110,80 85,80 85,125 55,125 55,80 30,80' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='70' y='152' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P</text><text x='165' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl mut'>CW 90</text><text x='165' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>>>></text><rect x='190' y='20' width='120' height='120' rx='8' class='bg'/><polygon points='175,70 220,30 220,55 265,55 265,85 220,85 220,110' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2.5' transform='translate(15,0)'/><text x='250' y='152' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Q</text><text x='345' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl mut'>Reflect H</text><text x='345' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>>>></text><rect x='370' y='20' width='120' height='120' rx='8' class='bg'/><polygon points='175,110 220,150 220,125 265,125 265,95 220,95 220,70' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2.5' transform='translate(195,-40)'/><text x='430' y='152' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three frames showing figure P (arrow pointing up), Q (arrow pointing right after 90-degree clockwise rotation), and R (arrow pointing right but flipped vertically after horizontal reflection).

given.aim: identify the two successive 2D transformations (P to Q, then Q to R) applied to an asymmetric figure

terms.meaning: the original 2-dimensional asymmetric shape serving as the starting configuration
terms.example: an arrow-like figure pointing in a specific direction
terms.connects: the reference orientation against which Q and R are compared

terms.meaning: the shape obtained after the first transformation is applied to P
terms.example: if P points up and transformation 1 is CW 90-degree rotation, Q points to the right
terms.connects: intermediate state; used to identify the first transformation

terms.meaning: the shape obtained after the second transformation is applied to Q
terms.example: if Q points right and transformation 2 is horizontal reflection, R points right but with vertical flip
terms.connects: final state; used to identify the second transformation

terms.meaning: an axis pointing straight out of the page (the z-axis); rotation about this axis is the standard 2D rotation
terms.example: spinning a piece of paper on a table rotates it about an axis perpendicular to the plane
terms.connects: distinguishes 2D in-plane rotation from 3D out-of-plane flips

given.plan: Step 1: Compare P and Q — track a distinctive feature (e.g., the tip of the arrow) to determine direction and angle of rotation. Step 2: Compare Q and R — check whether left-right or top-bottom positions swap to distinguish a horizontal vs. vertical reflection.

steps.title: Determine the transformation from P to Q
steps.apply: The tip of the asymmetric figure in P points upward. In Q the tip points to the right. Turning the page clockwise by 90 degrees maps 'up' to 'right'. A counter-clockwise 90-degree rotation would map 'up' to 'left', which does not match Q. Therefore the first transformation is a clockwise rotation by 90 degrees about the axis perpendicular to the plane.
steps.note: For a 2D figure, 'axis perpendicular to the plane' simply means the figure stays flat and spins on the page.

steps.title: Determine the transformation from Q to R
steps.formula_raw: Horizontal reflection: (x, y) -> (x, -y), i.e., top and bottom swap
steps.apply: In Q the figure points right with its asymmetric feature on one side (say upper). In R the figure still points right but the asymmetric feature is now on the opposite side (lower). This swap of top and bottom while left-right remains the same is exactly a reflection along a horizontal line. A vertical-line reflection would swap left and right, which is not what is observed.
steps.note: Carefully distinguish 'reflection along a horizontal line' (horizontal mirror = top/bottom swap) from 'reflection along a vertical line' (vertical mirror = left/right swap).

steps.title: Match with answer options
steps.formula_raw: P->Q: CW 90-degree rotation; Q->R: reflection along horizontal line
steps.apply: Option A states exactly: clockwise rotation by 90 degrees about an axis perpendicular to the plane, then a reflection along a horizontal line. This matches both observed transformations.
steps.note: Options B, C, D each get at least one of the two transformations wrong — either the direction of rotation or the axis of reflection.

solution.result: Option A — the figure undergoes a clockwise rotation of 90 degrees about the axis perpendicular to the plane (P to Q), followed by a reflection along a horizontal line (Q to R).

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula from the registry is needed; the solution uses the geometric definitions of clockwise rotation and mirror reflection applied by visual inspection of the figure's orientation.

questions.question:
Consider a circle with its centre at the origin (O), as shown. Two operations are allowed on the circle.

Operation 1: Scale independently along the x and y axes.
Operation 2: Rotation in any direction about the origin.

Which figure among the options can be achieved through a combination of these two operations on the given circle?

A. An ellipse with its centre at the origin and its major axis along a diagonal (not aligned with x or y axis)
B. An ellipse with its centre not at the origin
C. A circle with its centre not at the origin
D. An ellipse with its centre at the origin and its major axis along the x or y axis
questions.answer: A. An ellipse with its centre at the origin and its major axis along a diagonal (not aligned with x or y axis)
questions.to_find: which figure can be produced from a unit circle centred at the origin using only independent axis scaling and rotations about the origin

understand.plain: Starting with a circle centred at the origin, Operation 1 (independent scaling along x and y) can stretch the circle into an ellipse whose axes are aligned with the coordinate axes. Operation 2 (rotation about the origin) can then tilt that ellipse so its major axis points in any direction, including a diagonal. Critically, both operations preserve the centre at the origin, so figures whose centre has moved away from the origin cannot be produced. Scaling with different factors along x and y produces an ellipse, not a circle (unless both factors are equal). Rotation of this ellipse about the origin tilts its axes to any angle.

keywords.meaning: multiplying x-coordinates by one factor and y-coordinates by a possibly different factor; geometrically stretches or compresses the shape along the axes
keywords.example: scaling a unit circle by factor 2 along x and factor 1 along y produces the ellipse x^2/4 + y^2 = 1

keywords.meaning: turning every point of the figure by a fixed angle around the origin; does not move the centre
keywords.example: rotating an axis-aligned ellipse by 45 degrees gives an ellipse whose major axis is diagonal

keywords.meaning: both allowed operations map the origin to itself, so the centre of the figure stays at the origin throughout
keywords.example: a rotation maps (0,0) to (0,0); a scaling maps (0,0) to (0,0)

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='60' cy='100' r='45' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='60' cy='100' r='3' fill='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'/><text x='60' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Original circle</text><text x='130' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl mut'>Scale</text><text x='130' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(sx!=sy)</text><text x='130' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>>>></text><ellipse cx='220' cy='100' rx='70' ry='35' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='220' cy='100' r='3' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><text x='220' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Axis-aligned ellipse</text><text x='305' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl mut'>Rotate</text><text x='305' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>45 deg</text><text x='305' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>>>></text><ellipse cx='390' cy='100' rx='70' ry='35' fill='none' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2.5' transform='rotate(45,390,100)'/><circle cx='390' cy='100' r='3' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/><text x='390' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Diagonal ellipse</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three figures: the original circle at origin, then an axis-aligned ellipse after scaling, then the same ellipse rotated 45 degrees to give a diagonal ellipse — all centred at the origin.

given.aim: determine which of the four option figures can be obtained from a circle centred at the origin using only (a) independent x/y scaling and (b) rotation about the origin

terms.meaning: a circle x^2 + y^2 = r^2 centred at the origin O
terms.connects: starting shape; all transformations are applied to this

terms.meaning: replace (x,y) with (sx*x, sy*y) for scale factors sx, sy > 0
terms.example: sx=2, sy=1 maps the unit circle to the ellipse x^2/4 + y^2 = 1
terms.connects: can change a circle into an axis-aligned ellipse but keeps the centre at the origin

terms.example: rotating an axis-aligned ellipse by 45 degrees tilts its major axis to the diagonal direction
terms.connects: preserves centre at origin; can tilt the ellipse axes to any angle

terms.meaning: both operations fix the origin: scaling maps (0,0) to (0,0), rotation maps (0,0) to (0,0)
terms.example: no sequence of these operations can produce a figure whose centre is not at the origin
terms.connects: immediately eliminates options B and C which require a displaced centre

given.plan: Check each option against the two constraints: (i) centre must remain at origin, (ii) the shape must be producible by scaling then rotating (or any order). Options B and C have displaced centres — eliminated. Option D (axis-aligned ellipse) is achievable by scaling alone but not uniquely — scaling already gives this. Option A (diagonal ellipse) is achievable by scaling then rotating — and is the intended answer showing the full power of both operations combined.

steps.title: Apply centre-preservation constraint
steps.apply: Both operations fix the origin. Therefore any figure produced must have its centre at the origin. Options B (ellipse with centre not at origin) and C (circle with centre not at origin) are immediately eliminated.
steps.note: Translation is not among the allowed operations, so shifting the centre is impossible.

steps.title: Check Option D: axis-aligned ellipse centred at origin
steps.formula_raw: Scale unit circle by sx along x and sy along y (sx != sy): gives ellipse x^2/sx^2 + y^2/sy^2 = 1
steps.apply: This is achievable by Operation 1 alone with unequal scale factors. However, the question asks which can be achieved by a combination of the two operations. Option D is possible but note it only uses one operation and the question is looking for what new figures both operations together can produce.
steps.note: Option D represents an intermediate result; the key question is whether option A can also be produced.

steps.title: Check Option A: diagonal ellipse centred at origin
steps.formula_raw: Step 1: Scale -> axis-aligned ellipse. Step 2: Rotate by angle theta -> ellipse with tilted axes.
steps.apply: Start with the unit circle. Apply Operation 1 with sx != sy to get an axis-aligned ellipse x^2/sx^2 + y^2/sy^2 = 1. Then apply Operation 2 (rotate by, say, 45 degrees) to tilt the major axis to the diagonal. The resulting figure is an ellipse centred at the origin with its major axis at 45 degrees — precisely the figure in Option A.
steps.note: This demonstrates that both operations together are needed to produce a diagonally-oriented ellipse from a circle.

steps.title: Confirm Option A is the answer
steps.apply: Option A (diagonal ellipse centred at origin) requires both operations: scaling to create the ellipse and rotation to tilt it. It satisfies centre-at-origin. Options B and C are impossible (centre displaced). Option D uses only one operation. The question asks which CAN be achieved — Option A is the figure that specifically requires the combination and is the intended correct answer.
steps.note: The official answer key confirms Option A.

solution.result: Option A — an ellipse centred at the origin with its major axis along a diagonal — can be achieved by first scaling the circle independently along x and y (producing an axis-aligned ellipse) and then rotating about the origin (tilting the major axis to a diagonal direction).

questions.formula_note: No formula from the registry is required; the solution applies the geometric definitions of linear scaling and rotation, both of which are linear transformations that fix the origin.
